Category: --- RISK FRAMEWORK: the core entities and structures for identifying and tracking project risks ---

Description: An identified potential event or condition that could affect project objectives — the fundamental unit of risk management. Defined by source (what causes it), trigger (what signals it), consequence (what it affects), and owner (who manages it). Risks exist along a spectrum from known-known (identified and quantified) to unknown-unknown (emergent). In construction, most risks are known but unmanaged — the gap is process, not knowledge.
Industry Standard: PMI PMBOK Risk Management; ISO 31000; AACE 62R-11
Common Values: Design conflict, material shortage, permit delay, subcontractor default, weather event, unforeseen condition, scope creep, labor dispute
Source Systems: Risk registers (Excel/Smartsheet); P6 Risk; @Risk; Safran Risk; ARM; Oracle Primavera Risk Analysis
Procore Coverage: None — no native risk management tool. Risks are inferred from signals: change_events (scope), daily_log_delay (schedule/material), observations (safety/quality), incidents (safety events).
Benchmarking Use: Compare risk frequency and type distribution across project types and value bands
Notes: Risk management in construction is overwhelmingly informal — spreadsheets, meeting discussions, and institutional knowledge. The UCDM can infer risks from structured signals across domains even without a formal risk register.

Description: A project-level inventory of all identified risks — the master document that tracks risk identification, assessment, response, and status throughout the project lifecycle. Maintained from preconstruction through closeout. Typically a spreadsheet with columns for description, category, likelihood, impact, score, owner, response, status, and trigger.
Common Values: 50–200 risks per major project; reviewed monthly or at phase transitions
Source Systems: Excel; Smartsheet; P6 Risk; ARM; Safran; Oracle Primavera Risk Analysis; custom SharePoint lists
Procore Coverage: None — no native risk register. Closest proxy: aggregating change events, RFIs, observations, and delays into a risk-like view.
Benchmarking Use: Compare risk register size and category distribution across project types
Notes: Most GCs maintain risk registers in Excel. The opportunity is to auto-populate risk signals from Procore data into an external register — or build one natively.

Description: A classification that groups risks by their source or nature — the organizing principle for a risk register. Categories are typically defined at the company or program level and applied consistently across projects.
Industry Standard: PMI PMBOK Risk Breakdown Structure (RBS); AACE 62R-11
Common Values: Design, Construction, Financial, Schedule, Safety, Environmental, Regulatory, Market, Geotechnical, Labor, Coordination, Political, Force Majeure
Source Systems: Risk registers; project management plans; insurance policies
Procore Coverage: None — no structured risk categorization. Procore signals map to categories: change_reason → design/scope, delay_type → schedule/material, observation.category → safety/quality, incident → safety.
Benchmarking Use: Compare risk category frequency across projects to identify systemic exposure patterns
Notes: Risk categories should map to the UCDM's cross-domain relationships: design risk → Documents & Communications (RFIs), financial risk → Financial Instruments (change orders), safety risk → Quality & Safety Records (observations, incidents).

Description: An observable condition or event that signals a risk is materializing — the early warning system. Triggers convert abstract risks into actionable alerts. In construction, triggers are often embedded in daily operations data: an RFI surge signals design risk, a submittal delay signals supply chain risk, recurring safety observations signal hazard risk.
Common Values: RFI volume spike, submittal delay, weather forecast, change order rate increase, safety observation recurrence, labor shortage report, permit delay notice
Source Systems: Risk registers; project dashboards; ERP alerts; Procore (via signal analysis)
Procore Coverage: Low — no native trigger mechanism, but underlying data exists: RFI counts, submittal overdue flags, delay logs, observation patterns. Triggers must be computed from these signals.
Benchmarking Use: Compare trigger frequency and lead time to risk materialization across projects
Notes: Triggers are the bridge between reactive and proactive risk management. Procore's cross-domain data (RFIs + delays + observations + changes) is uniquely positioned to detect triggers that single-domain tools miss.

Description: The person or party responsible for monitoring a risk and executing the response plan — accountability for risk management. In construction, risk ownership follows contractual responsibility: the GC owns means-and-methods risk, the architect owns design risk, the owner owns market/financing risk, subcontractors own trade-specific execution risk.
Industry Standard: PMI PMBOK 11.5; AIA A201 (risk allocation); ConsensusDocs
Common Values: Project Manager, Superintendent, Estimator, Architect, Owner's Rep, Subcontractor PM, Safety Manager
Source Systems: Risk registers; contract documents; meeting minutes
Procore Coverage: None — no native risk ownership tracking. Project role memberships (project_role_memberships table) identify who holds PM, Super, Safety roles but not risk-specific assignments.
Benchmarking Use: Compare risk ownership distribution across project delivery methods
Notes: Risk ownership often misaligns with contractual responsibility — the party best positioned to manage a risk is not always the party contractually responsible for it.

Category: --- RISK ASSESSMENT: how risks are evaluated for likelihood, impact, and priority ---

Description: The evaluation of probability that a risk event will occur — qualitative (High/Medium/Low) or quantitative (percentage, frequency). In construction, likelihood is often estimated from historical data: how often has this happened on similar projects? OSHA incident rates, change order frequency, weather patterns, and subcontractor performance history all inform likelihood.
Common Values: Very Low (<10%), Low (10-25%), Medium (25-50%), High (50-75%), Very High (>75%); or 1-5 scale
Source Systems: Risk registers; Monte Carlo tools (@Risk, Crystal Ball, Primavera Risk); actuarial models
Procore Coverage: None — no native likelihood assessment. Historical rates can be computed from Procore data: incident frequency, change order rate, delay frequency per project type.
Benchmarking Use: Benchmark likelihood by risk category and project type to improve estimation accuracy
Notes: Qualitative likelihood (H/M/L) is standard in construction. Quantitative likelihood is rare outside of large infrastructure projects where Monte Carlo simulation justifies the effort.

Description: The evaluation of consequence severity if a risk materializes — measured across one or more dimensions: cost, schedule, quality, safety, reputation. Impact is the 'so what' of risk — a likely event with negligible impact is low priority, while an unlikely event with catastrophic impact (structural failure, fatality) demands active management regardless of probability.
Industry Standard: PMI PMBOK 11.3; AACE 62R-11; OSHA severity classifications
Common Values: Cost impact ($K–$M range); schedule impact (days/weeks); safety severity (first aid → fatality); quality impact (rework scope)
Source Systems: Risk registers; cost estimates; schedule analysis tools; insurance actuarial tables
Procore Coverage: Low — incident_record.estimated_cost_impact exists for safety events. Change event line items provide cost impact for scope risks. No native multi-dimensional impact scoring.
Benchmarking Use: Compare actual impact by risk category to improve future impact estimation
Notes: Impact assessment is most accurate when grounded in historical data from similar projects — exactly where Procore's cross-company benchmarking creates value.

Description: The combined measure of likelihood × impact that determines risk priority — the basis for triage and resource allocation. Typically displayed as a number (1–25 on a 5×5 matrix) or color (red/amber/green). In construction, risk scores drive which risks get contingency, which get mitigation plans, and which are accepted and monitored.
Common Values: 1–5 (low) to 20–25 (critical) on 5×5 matrix; or qualitative H/M/L
Source Systems: Risk registers; risk matrices; Monte Carlo output (expected value)
Procore Coverage: None — no native risk scoring. Could be computed from Procore signals: frequency × average cost impact from historical change events, incidents, delays.
Benchmarking Use: Compare risk score distributions across projects to identify high-risk patterns
Notes: Risk scores are only as good as the underlying likelihood and impact estimates. Procore data enables evidence-based scoring rather than gut-feel estimates.

Description: A visual grid that plots risks by likelihood (y-axis) and impact (x-axis) — the standard tool for risk prioritization. Typically 3×3 or 5×5 with color-coded zones (green = accept, amber = mitigate, red = escalate). The matrix is a communication tool — it makes risk priorities visible to the project team and stakeholders.
Common Values: 3×3, 4×4, or 5×5 grid; color-coded (green/amber/red); quadrant-based prioritization
Source Systems: Risk registers (typically embedded); Safran Risk; @Risk; project dashboards; PowerPoint/Excel
Procore Coverage: None — no native risk matrix visualization. Could be generated from computed risk scores overlaid on a standard grid template.
Benchmarking Use: Compare risk matrix density and distribution patterns across project portfolios
Notes: The risk matrix is the most widely recognized risk management artifact in construction — even teams without formal risk processes understand the red/amber/green framework.

Category: --- RISK RESPONSE: how risks are addressed once identified and assessed ---

Description: The strategic approach to managing an identified risk — the four fundamental options. Each risk in the register should have an assigned response type that determines the nature of the mitigation plan.
Common Values: Avoid (eliminate the risk by changing plans), Mitigate (reduce likelihood or impact), Transfer (shift to another party via contract/insurance/bond), Accept (monitor without active response)
Source Systems: Risk registers; contract documents; insurance policies; meeting minutes
Procore Coverage: None — no native response tracking. Response types are implicit in actions: change orders (avoid/mitigate), insurance (transfer), contingency (accept).
Benchmarking Use: Compare response type distribution by risk category across projects
Notes: In construction, Transfer is the dominant strategy — subcontracting is fundamentally risk transfer. The GC transfers execution risk to subs while retaining coordination risk.

Description: A specific planned action to reduce the likelihood or impact of an identified risk — the operational response. Mitigation actions have owners, due dates, status, and cost. Examples: early procurement for supply chain risk, additional shoring for geotechnical risk, design review for coordination risk, safety training for hazard risk.
Common Values: Corrective action, preventive action, design modification, schedule acceleration, additional inspection, training, mock-up, prototype, early procurement
Source Systems: Risk registers; action item trackers; meeting minutes; Procore (incident_action for safety)
Procore Coverage: Low — incident_action table tracks corrective/preventive actions for safety incidents (action_type_name, observation_id link). No general risk mitigation tracking.
Benchmarking Use: Compare mitigation completion rate and effectiveness across project types
Notes: Incident actions in Procore link to observations (observation_id) — this creates a closed-loop safety risk mitigation path. General risk mitigation has no equivalent tracking.

Description: A budget allocation held in reserve for identified risks — financial buffer against cost uncertainty. Contingency is typically 3-10% of project cost depending on project complexity and phase (higher in early phases, drawn down as risks are resolved or materialized). Distinct from allowances (placeholder for undefined scope) and management reserve (for unknown-unknowns).
Common Values: 3-5% for well-defined projects; 5-10% for complex/renovation; 10-15%+ for early estimates; drawn down through change orders and direct costs
Source Systems: Cost estimates; budgets; project controls software; Sage 300; Viewpoint; CMiC; Excel
Procore Coverage: Low — budget_line_items may contain contingency allocations via budget_code, but no structured contingency tracking. No burn-down or risk-linkage.
Benchmarking Use: Compare contingency as % of total value and burn rate by project type and phase
Notes: Contingency management is one of the least standardized practices in construction. Some owners hold contingency; some GCs hold it; some split it. The budget_code signal for 'Contingency' in Procore is the best available proxy.

Description: Time reserves built into the project schedule to absorb schedule risk — the temporal equivalent of contingency reserve. Buffers may be explicit (milestone buffers between phases) or implicit (float in non-critical paths). Critical Chain Project Management (CCPM) formalizes buffer management.
Industry Standard: AACE 10S-90; PMI PMBOK Schedule Management; CCPM (Goldratt)
Common Values: 5-15% of activity duration as buffers; milestone-to-milestone gaps of 1-4 weeks; total float on non-critical paths
Source Systems: P6; MS Project; Asta Powerproject; Phoenix; schedule analysis tools
Procore Coverage: Low — schedule_tasks table has float and duration but no native buffer identification or tracking. Buffer consumption must be inferred from schedule updates.
Benchmarking Use: Compare total float erosion rate and milestone slippage across project types
Notes: In practice, schedule buffers are consumed silently — contractors use float without explicit tracking. Float erosion analysis from Procore schedule data can reveal hidden buffer consumption.

Description: The remaining risk exposure after mitigation actions are applied — the risk that persists despite response efforts. Residual risk may be accepted, monitored, or require additional response. It is the actual exposure the project carries, not the initial identified exposure.
Common Values: Re-scored risk after mitigation (lower likelihood and/or impact); monitored via risk register updates
Source Systems: Risk registers; project status reports; quarterly reviews
Procore Coverage: None — no native residual risk tracking. Could be inferred by comparing pre/post-mitigation incident rates or change order frequency.
Benchmarking Use: Compare residual risk levels by response type to assess mitigation effectiveness
Notes: Residual risk is rarely tracked formally in construction. Most teams re-assess risks qualitatively at milestone reviews rather than quantitatively re-scoring.

Category: --- RISK CATEGORIES: the types of risk encountered in construction projects, organized by source ---

Description: Risk arising from incomplete, conflicting, or evolving design — the most common source of change orders in construction. Design risk manifests as RFIs (clarification needed), design changes (revisions), coordination conflicts (clashes between disciplines), and scope growth (owner-driven additions). Design risk is highest in design-bid-build delivery and lowest in design-build.
Common Values: Incomplete drawings, conflicting details, late design decisions, owner scope changes, code interpretation differences, constructability issues
Source Systems: RFI logs; drawing revision tracking; BIM coordination; change management systems; Procore
Procore Coverage: Medium — change_events.change_reason captures design_development (354K CEs, 6,748 cos, $54.8B). rfis.predicted_topic provides ML-classified design topics (176 categories, 17,540 cos). coordination_issues for BIM conflicts.
Benchmarking Use: Compare design change rate (% of total changes) and RFI volume by project type and delivery method
Notes: Procore signals for design risk are strong: change_reason + RFI predicted_topic + coordination_issues provide three independent detection surfaces. See Insight #31 validation.

Description: Risk from means and methods, productivity, sequencing, and trade coordination — the risk of building it wrong or slow. Includes rework (built incorrectly, must redo), productivity loss (slower than planned), sequencing errors (wrong order), and coordination failures (trades interfering with each other). Owned primarily by the GC and subcontractors.
Common Values: Rework, out-of-sequence work, trade stacking, access conflicts, equipment failure, crew productivity variance, material waste
Source Systems: Field logs; productivity tracking; quality inspections; Procore; Fieldwire; PlanGrid
Procore Coverage: Medium — observations (quality category) detect during-construction issues. Punch items detect closeout deficiencies. Daily log notes capture field activity. inspection_items track checklist compliance.
Benchmarking Use: Compare rework rate and punch item density by project type and trade
Notes: Execution risk signals in Procore: observation.category='Quality' (5,923 cos), punch_item.name (11,722 cos), inspection_items.status='Deficient'. See Insights #1 and #4.

Description: Risk of cost overruns, cash flow problems, payment disputes, or contractor default — the money risks. Includes budget variance (actual > plan), cash flow gaps (invoices outpacing payments), subcontractor financial health (inability to complete), and owner funding risk (delayed payments or financing failures).
Industry Standard: AACE 62R-11; AIA A201 (payment provisions); Miller Act (federal bond requirements)
Common Values: Budget overrun, change order disputes, payment delays, subcontractor default, material price escalation, retainage disputes, lien claims
Source Systems: ERP systems (Sage, Viewpoint, CMiC); Procore financials; project controls; construction lending platforms
Procore Coverage: Medium — budget_line_items provide planned vs. actual variance. Change events and CCOs track cost growth. Vendor insurance tracking monitors subcontractor financial compliance. No cash flow forecasting.
Benchmarking Use: Compare cost growth % and change order rate by project type and value band
Notes: Financial risk detection in Procore relies on change order rate, budget variance, and invoice timing — all available but require computed metrics, not raw fields.

Description: Risk of delays and timeline overruns from any cause — weather, permits, material delivery, labor shortages, design changes, differing site conditions. Schedule risk compounds: a 2-week delay to one trade can cascade into months of impact through downstream dependencies. Critical path analysis identifies which risks have the most schedule exposure.
Industry Standard: AACE 62R-11; AACE 29R-03 (forensic schedule analysis); PMI PMBOK Schedule Management
Common Values: Weather delays, permit delays, material delivery delays, labor shortages, design change delays, differing site conditions, equipment breakdown
Source Systems: P6; MS Project; Procore; Asta Powerproject; Tilos; lean scheduling tools
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily_log_delay.delay_type provides structured categories (Weather, Material, Owner Directive, etc. — 84K records for Material alone). schedule_task_changes tracks schedule updates. daily_log_observed_weather_condition records weather.
Benchmarking Use: Compare delay frequency and duration by delay type and project type
Notes: Procore's delay log is one of the strongest schedule risk signals in the industry — structured delay_type with duration and comments. Most competitors have unstructured delay tracking.

Description: Risk of injuries, fatalities, and safety incidents — the most regulated and consequential risk category. Safety risk is measured by incident rates (TRIR, DART, EMR), managed through hazard identification and mitigation, and regulated by OSHA. In construction, the 'Fatal Four' (falls, struck-by, electrocution, caught-in/between) account for most fatalities.
Industry Standard: OSHA 29 CFR 1926; ANSI Z10; ISO 45001; OSHA Fatal Four
Common Values: Fall hazard, struck-by, electrical, caught-in/between, trenching/excavation, scaffolding, crane operations, confined space
Source Systems: Procore; Safety management platforms (SafetyCulture, Predictive Solutions, SmartTagIt); OSHA logs; insurance carriers
Procore Coverage: High — incident table with hazard, contributing_behavior, contributing_condition, recordable flag. incident_record with injury details, severity, work days lost. observation.hazard_name for OSHA-aligned hazard taxonomy (16 types, 1,296 cos). daily_log_safety_violation.
Benchmarking Use: Compare TRIR and hazard recurrence rates by project type and trade
Notes: Safety is Procore's strongest risk data domain. Incident + observation + inspection create a three-layer safety risk detection system. See Insight #11 for recurrence patterns.

Description: Risk from contamination, hazardous materials, spills, weather events, and environmental compliance — both site-condition risk (what's already there) and operational risk (what the project creates). Includes asbestos/lead discovery, soil contamination, stormwater violations, and environmental incidents.
Industry Standard: EPA regulations; SWPPP requirements; CERCLA; state environmental laws
Common Values: Soil contamination, asbestos/lead abatement, stormwater violations, spill events, endangered species, wetland impacts, air quality violations
Source Systems: Environmental monitoring systems; SWPPP software; incident management; Procore
Procore Coverage: Medium — incident_record.incident_environmental_type tracks environmental incidents with quantity and unit of measure. observation.category='Environmental' captures field environmental observations. No SWPPP or remediation tracking.
Benchmarking Use: Compare environmental incident rate by project type and geography
Notes: Environmental risk is often the highest-consequence risk on renovation and industrial projects but has the lowest data maturity in construction software.

Description: Risk from permitting delays, code changes, inspection failures, and regulatory enforcement — the risk that authorities block or delay work. Includes building permit delays, failed AHJ inspections, zoning issues, utility connection delays, and code interpretation disputes.
Industry Standard: IBC; IRC; NFPA; local building codes; zoning ordinances; ADA
Common Values: Permit delay, failed inspection, code change mid-project, zoning variance denial, utility connection delay, fire marshal rejection
Source Systems: Permit tracking software; city portals; project management systems; Procore (limited)
Procore Coverage: Low — no native permit tracking. Inspection_items can track code compliance on checklists. Daily_log_delay.delay_type may capture regulatory delays as custom values. Most permit data lives in city portals and email.
Benchmarking Use: Compare permit timeline by jurisdiction and project type
Notes: Permit risk is critical-path but data-poor in construction software. The data lives in municipal systems, not project management tools.

Description: Risk from material shortages, delivery delays, price escalation, and sole-source dependencies — the risk that what you need isn't available when you need it. Amplified by global supply chain disruptions, long-lead procurement, and just-in-time delivery practices common in construction.
Common Values: Material shortage, delivery delay, price escalation, sole-source vulnerability, fabrication delay, shipping disruption, tariff impact
Source Systems: Procurement systems; ERP (Sage, Viewpoint); submittal logs; delivery tracking; Procore
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily_log_delay.delay_type='Material' (84K records, 4,351 cos). submittals.overdue flag (718K records). Submittal delivery dates too sparse (1%) for gap model. See Material Shortage validation.
Benchmarking Use: Compare material delay frequency and duration by material type and project type
Notes: Procore's material delay signal (daily_log_delay) is structured and high-volume. Combined with submittal overdue tracking, it provides two independent supply chain risk indicators.

Description: Risk from unforeseen subsurface conditions — the most unpredictable risk in construction. Includes unexpected soil conditions, groundwater, contamination, underground utilities, and rock. Geotechnical risk is highest on renovation, infrastructure, and greenfield sites without adequate investigation. Differing site conditions claims are among the most litigated in construction.
Industry Standard: ASCE 21 (Geotechnical Baseline Reports); ASTM soil testing standards; AIA A201 differing conditions clause
Common Values: Unexpected rock, high water table, contaminated soil, unknown utilities, settlement, unstable slopes, sinkholes
Source Systems: Geotechnical reports; boring logs; CPT data; GIS systems; site investigation software
Procore Coverage: None — no native geotechnical data. Daily log notes may reference soil conditions. Change events with 'unforeseen conditions' reason capture cost impact after discovery.
Benchmarking Use: Compare differing site condition change order frequency by project type and geography
Notes: Geotechnical risk is the classic 'unknown unknown' — by definition, the data doesn't exist until discovery. Pre-construction geotech reports are typically PDFs outside construction software.

Description: Risk from workforce availability, productivity variance, jurisdictional disputes, and labor relations — the people risk. Includes skilled labor shortages, productivity below plan, trade jurisdictional conflicts, union actions, and key-person dependency. Labor risk is structural in construction due to the aging workforce and project-based employment model.
Industry Standard: Bureau of Labor Statistics; union agreements; prevailing wage requirements; Davis-Bacon Act
Common Values: Skilled labor shortage, productivity loss, jurisdictional dispute, union work rules, absenteeism, key-person departure, overtime burnout
Source Systems: Workforce management systems; timekeeping; daily logs; labor reports; Procore
Procore Coverage: Low — daily_log_manpower records crew counts and trade_name. No native labor availability, productivity measurement, or jurisdictional tracking. Workforce schema tables exist but are NOT READY for import.
Benchmarking Use: Compare manpower loading and productivity rates by trade and project type
Notes: Labor risk is the #1 concern for US contractors but the least digitized risk category. Most labor data lives in timekeeping systems separate from project management.

Description: Risk from trade interference, design conflicts, and inadequate planning — the risk that parallel workstreams collide. Includes spatial conflicts (two trades in the same space), temporal conflicts (wrong sequence), and information conflicts (outdated drawings). BIM coordination and pull planning are the primary mitigation tools.
Industry Standard: BIM Execution Plan; AGC Lean Construction guidelines; ICE sessions
Common Values: Trade stacking, spatial conflicts, above-ceiling congestion, MEP coordination failures, design-field discrepancies, RFI-driven rework
Source Systems: BIM tools (Navisworks, Solibri, BIM 360); pull planning boards; Procore (limited)
Procore Coverage: Low — coordination_issues table exists (17,999 design-typed, 327 cos) with issue_type and rfi_header_id link. Daily log notes may reference coordination. BIM data lives in Navisworks/Solibri, not Procore.
Benchmarking Use: Compare coordination issue density by project complexity and MEP scope
Notes: Coordination risk increases nonlinearly with project complexity. MEP-heavy projects (hospitals, data centers, labs) have the highest coordination risk.

Category: --- RISK TRANSFER & ALLOCATION: how risk is contractually managed between parties ---

Description: The contractual obligation for project participants to carry specific insurance coverages — the financial backstop for risk transfer. Standard requirements include General Liability, Workers' Compensation, Umbrella/Excess, Builders Risk, Professional Liability (for designers), and Pollution coverage. Insurance compliance is tracked at both company and project levels.
Industry Standard: AIA A201 (insurance provisions); ConsensusDocs; state WC requirements; contract specifications
Common Values: General Liability, Workers' Comp, Umbrella/Excess, Builders Risk, Professional Liability, Pollution, Auto, Property
Source Systems: Insurance tracking platforms; Procore; PINS; myCOI; certificate management systems
Procore Coverage: High — company_vendor_insurances and project_vendor_insurances tables track policy_number, insurance_type, insurance_provider, effective/expiration dates, limit_amounts, compliance status, and expiration notifications. Structured and widely adopted.
Benchmarking Use: Compare insurance compliance rates and coverage gaps by vendor type and project size
Notes: Procore's insurance tracking is one of the most complete risk transfer data sets in the platform — structured types, amounts, dates, and compliance status.

Description: A three-party guarantee that a contractor will fulfill contractual obligations — bid bonds (guarantee bid price), performance bonds (guarantee completion), and payment bonds (guarantee subcontractor/supplier payment). Required on all federal projects (Miller Act) and most public projects. Less common on private work.
Industry Standard: Miller Act (federal); Little Miller Acts (state); AIA A312 bond forms
Common Values: Bid bond (5-10% of bid), Performance bond (100% of contract), Payment bond (100% of contract), Maintenance bond
Source Systems: Surety companies; bond tracking systems; contract management; Procore (limited)
Procore Coverage: None — no native bond tracking. Contract-level bond requirements may be noted in commitment descriptions or custom fields. Insurance tables track insurance but not surety bonds specifically.
Benchmarking Use: Compare bond rates and claim frequency by contractor size and project type
Notes: Bonds are a critical risk transfer mechanism but poorly tracked in project management software. Most bond data lives in contract documents and surety company systems.

Description: The contractual assignment of specific risks to parties — who bears the consequence when a risk materializes. Risk allocation is defined in contract terms and varies by delivery method. Design-bid-build allocates design risk to the owner/architect; design-build shifts it to the contractor. Differing site conditions, force majeure, and schedule liquidated damages are common allocation provisions.
Industry Standard: AIA A201; ConsensusDocs 200; EJCDC; DBIA; AGC Contract Guide
Common Values: Owner-retained risks (permitting, financing, code changes), Contractor-retained risks (means/methods, safety, productivity), Shared risks (weather, unforeseen conditions, force majeure)
Source Systems: Contract documents; legal review; project management plans; risk registers
Procore Coverage: None — risk allocation lives in contract language, not structured data. Contract terms are typically PDF/Word documents, not database fields.
Benchmarking Use: Compare risk allocation patterns by delivery method and project type
Notes: Risk allocation is the most consequential and least digitized aspect of risk management. It determines who pays when things go wrong — and it's all in contract prose.

Category: --- STANDARD MEASURES: KPIs and metrics derived from risk domain entities ---

Description: Total financial exposure from identified risks — sum of (likelihood × cost impact) across all risks in the register. Provides a single number representing the project's aggregate risk in dollar terms. Used to justify contingency levels and prioritize mitigation spending.
Common Values: Typically 5-15% of project value for active risk exposure
Source Systems: Risk registers; Monte Carlo simulation output; project controls
Procore Coverage: None — must be computed from external risk register data or inferred from Procore change/incident history.
Benchmarking Use: Compare risk exposure as % of project value by project type and complexity

Description: Rate at which contingency reserve is consumed over project duration — actual contingency usage divided by elapsed project time. A leading indicator: fast burn early signals cost risk. Formula: cumulative contingency used / (elapsed duration / total planned duration). Healthy projects show burn rate below 1.0 through the first 75% of the project.
Common Values: Below 0.5 (healthy), 0.5-1.0 (monitor), above 1.0 (at risk)
Source Systems: Project controls; cost reports; budget tracking; ERP systems
Procore Coverage: Low — requires identifying contingency budget lines via budget_code, then tracking draws against them via change orders and budget modifications. Possible but not out-of-box.
Benchmarking Use: Compare contingency burn rate curves by project type and delivery method

Description: Total Recordable Incident Rate — the standard OSHA safety metric. Formula: (number of recordable incidents × 200,000) / total hours worked. Measures overall safety risk realization. DART rate (Days Away, Restricted, or Transferred) is the severity-weighted variant.
Common Values: Construction industry avg TRIR: 2.5-3.0; top performers: <1.0; DART: ~1.5 avg
Source Systems: OSHA 300 logs; safety management systems; Procore; insurance carriers
Procore Coverage: High — incident table has recordable flag. incident_record has work_days_absent, work_days_restricted, work_days_transferred for DART calculation. Hours worked requires external data (timekeeping).
Benchmarking Use: Compare TRIR and DART by project type and company to benchmark safety performance
Notes: TRIR is the single most important risk metric in construction. Procore has recordable flag and severity data; the gap is hours worked (denominator), which comes from timekeeping systems.

Description: Percentage of planned mitigation actions completed on time — measures the effectiveness of risk response execution. Formula: completed actions / total planned actions. Tracks whether the team is actually doing what they planned to manage risk.
Common Values: Above 80% (strong risk management), 60-80% (adequate), below 60% (risk management breakdown)
Source Systems: Risk registers; action trackers; meeting minutes; Procore (incident_action only)
Procore Coverage: Low — incident_action tracks safety-specific corrective actions with status. No general mitigation action tracking.
Benchmarking Use: Compare mitigation completion rates by risk category and project phase

Description: Percentage of original contract value added through change orders — a proxy for scope and design risk realization. Formula: total approved change order value / original contract value. Higher rates indicate more realized risk. Typical range 5-15% for well-managed projects.
Common Values: 5-10% (well-managed), 10-15% (typical), 15-25% (high risk realized), >25% (distressed)
Source Systems: Project controls; cost reports; Procore; ERP systems
Procore Coverage: High — change_events and commitment_change_orders provide complete change order data with amounts, reasons, and approval status. Deduplicate CE/CCO via GREATEST.
Benchmarking Use: Compare change order rate by project type and delivery method to benchmark scope risk

Description: Rate at which total float is consumed across the project schedule — a leading indicator of schedule risk. Formula: (baseline total float - current total float) / baseline total float. Rapid float erosion signals that the schedule is tightening and risk of delay is increasing.
Common Values: 0-20% consumed (healthy), 20-50% (monitor), >50% (critical)
Source Systems: P6; MS Project; Procore (limited); schedule analysis tools
Procore Coverage: Low — schedule_tasks has float data but limited baseline comparison capability. schedule_task_changes tracks updates but reason field is generic free text.
Benchmarking Use: Compare float erosion patterns by project phase and type
